title: Chapter 54: Vance's Endgame
content: The data stream was unlike anything Wren had touched before.

She'd been swimming through NeuroScale's peripheral systems for days, mapping their rebuilt infrastructure from the outside in. After the broadcast, after the amplification, the corporation had hardened its digital perimeter — military firewalls, quantum-encrypted internal networks, air-gapped servers in locations that didn't appear on any map. But they still had to communicate. Data still had to move. And wherever data moved, Wren could follow.
She found the project files at 2:13 AM, buried under three layers of misdirection and a file naming convention that was designed to look like routine pharmaceutical research.
Project RESONANCE.
The name made her skin prickle.
She opened the first file and the world tilted.
MK.II Hounds.

Not the clumsy biological predators she'd barely survived in the Underlayer — those had been prototypes, proof-of-concept demonstrations of what ANX-00 could create. The new generation was something else entirely.

Predictive AI cores. Neural networks trained on the movement patterns, digital signatures, and behavioral data of every known invisible subject. The MK.II Hounds wouldn't track by scent or sound — they would track by probability, calculating where an invisible person was most likely to be based on algorithmic models of their past behavior.

Wren read the specifications and felt something cold settle in her stomach. The old Hounds were dangerous but stupid — they could be outmaneuvered, confused, escaped. These would be neither. These would learn.

The development timeline was staggering. NeuroScale had begun work on the MK.II platform three months after the original Hounds failed in the field — Vance hadn't wasted a single day. The AI cores were built on an architecture stolen from a defense contractor, modified for civilian deployment, and trained on a dataset that included two years of surveillance footage from every NeuroScale facility worldwide. Every invisible who'd ever been captured on camera — however briefly, however partially — was in the training set.

Wren's own movement patterns were in there. She found them in the appendices: Subject 13 behavioral model, 847 data points, confidence interval 73%. They'd watched her for months, cataloguing the way she moved through spaces, the pauses she took, the routes she preferred. They didn't know where she was now, but they knew how she thought.
The Hounds weren't just predators. They were profilers.
But the Hounds weren't the worst of it.
The worst was on the next page.
COUNTER-SIGNAL. Device designation: CS-1.

A reversal mechanism. Wren read the technical documents three times, her mind struggling to process the implications. The Counter-Signal operated on the same frequency as the glitch — it was built from the same fundamental physics that made invisibility possible. But instead of amplifying the effect, it inverted it. Pushed the frequency back toward the visible spectrum. Forced the invisible to become seen.
The schematics showed a device roughly the size of a smartphone. Small enough to carry. Powerful enough to affect every amplified invisible within its operational radius.
And the operational radius, according to the projections, was measurable in kilometers.

The engineering details made her gorge rise. The CS-1 used a phased array of piezoelectric transducers to broadcast an interference pattern that disrupted the quantum-level coherence of the glitch frequency. In plain language: it un-made invisibility by screaming the opposite signal until the original couldn't sustain itself.

The power requirements were immense. The device needed a direct connection to a building's electrical grid or a portable fusion cell that didn't exist yet. But the schematics included a workaround — a parasitic coupling that drew power from the building's own infrastructure. From a server room. A transformer station. Anywhere that housed enough juice to feed the thing.
Which meant the Counter-Signal was designed to be deployed inside NeuroScale Tower. Fixed. Stationary. And once it was online, every amplified invisible in the city would light up like a flare.

Wren's hands trembled. She felt the network react — thirty-seven frequencies straining toward her distress like plants toward light. She pushed calming pulses back at them, but her own heartbeat was too loud, too fast, and they could all feel it.

If the Counter-Signal went online, every amplified invisible within range would become visible simultaneously. Stripped of their protection. Exposed. Vulnerable. Not just to the Hounds — to everyone. To a world that had spent years pretending they didn't exist, suddenly forced to acknowledge them, on terms that made them targets instead of people.
It wouldn't be visibility. It would be a hunt.
She kept digging. The deeper she went, the worse it got.

Personnel files. Deployment schedules. A memo from Vance herself, dated six weeks ago, authorizing the recall of all MK.I Hound units for "decommissioning and data extraction." Another memo, three days later, approving the transfer of Hound AI cores to the MK.II development team. Nothing wasted. Nothing thrown away. Even the failures were recycled.
And then — buried in a folder marked ARCHIVE, password-protected with a key that took Wren ninety seconds to crack — she found Vance's personal logs.
Not the corporate records. Not the sanitized board presentations. The private recordings. Voice memos, made late at night, when even Lena Vance let her guard down.
Wren hesitated. Then she pressed play.
Vance's voice filled the frequency, cool and measured, with that cello-string precision that made every word sound like it had been curated.

*"Subject 13 continues to exceed projections. The amplification event was... unforeseen. The network topology that emerged — I won't pretend I anticipated it. But adaptation is the hallmark of good science, and we will adapt."*
A pause. The sound of liquid being poured. Ice clinking against glass.

*"The Counter-Signal is the answer. Not destruction — revelation. I've spent fifteen years studying what happens when human beings cease to be perceived. They don't vanish. They transform. They become something the world isn't prepared for. And the world needs time to adjust. The CS-1 provides that time — it makes the invisible visible again, on our schedule, at our pace. Controlled visibility. Controlled integration."*
Another pause. Longer this time. When Vance spoke again, her voice had shifted — something underneath the composure, something Wren couldn't quite name.

*"I know what they'll say. That I'm a monster. That I created a disease and called it a cure. But they don't understand — the glitch isn't an aberration. It's the next step. The problem isn't the transformation. The problem is the world's inability to see what it's becoming. And if I have to force that vision on them — if I have to drag every invisible subject into the light so the world can finally acknowledge what exists — then that's what I'll do."*
The recording ended.

Wren sat with the silence for a long time. The thing that disturbed her most wasn't the ambition or the ruthlessness. It was the certainty. Vance believed she was right. Not in the way that tyrants believe they're right — in the way that surgeons believe they're right. She was cutting into the world and calling it healing, and she would not be dissuaded.

She pulled Ash into the Underlayer to show him. The space between frequencies, the place where they could exist together without the world's noise — it had become their war room, their sanctuary, the only place where touch was possible.
He read the files. His expression didn't change, but she felt the temperature of his frequency spike — white-hot rage compressed into stillness.
He listened to Vance's recording without a word. When it ended, his jaw was so tight she could see the muscles standing out beneath his skin.
"She thinks she's saving us," he said. Not a question.
"She thinks she's saving the world. We're just the instrument."
"Where?"

"NeuroScale Tower. Primary research facility. Sixty floors of the most secure corporate architecture ever built. The Counter-Signal is already there — it's being assembled on the fifty-ninth floor. Final integration in seven days."
"When?"
She checked the project timeline. The Counter-Signal was in final testing. Integration was scheduled for—
"Seven days. If it goes online, every invisible in this city becomes a target. And once the tower proves the concept works, they'll build more. Deploy them everywhere."
Ash absorbed this. The frequency between them crackled.
"Then we have seven days to take that tower."

"It's not that simple. The building is fortress-grade. Biometric checkpoints on every floor. Armed security. And the new Hounds — they've already deployed prototypes in the lower levels. The behavioral models they're using—"
She stopped. The implication hung between them.
"They're using our data," Ash said quietly. "Our patterns."
"Yes. They know how we move. How we think. The old playbook won't work."

"Then we don't use the old playbook." Ash's form flickered at the edges — not instability, but intensity. The glitch responding to his focus. "We've been hiding since the beginning. Moving through the cracks. Slipping past. That's how they expect us to operate. That's what their security is designed for — ghosts in the margins."
He stepped closer. The frequency between them hummed.
"We're not ghosts anymore, Wren. We're the glitch itself. We don't slip through the cracks. We ARE the cracks."
Wren felt the network listening. Thirty-seven people, scattered across the world, holding their breath through the frequency.
"You want to assault the tower."
"I want to make the tower assault itself."

She thought about it. The building's systems — its power grid, its network infrastructure, its surveillance apparatus, its climate control, its elevators, its locks. All digital. All connected. All vulnerable to someone who existed inside the frequency that connected them.
The tower was a machine. And she could become its malfunction.

"We'll need a team," she said. "Amplified. People who can move through the physical space while I handle the systems. Four, maybe five. More than that and the Hounds' predictive models start generating useful data."

"I know people," Ash said. "Invisibles who've been fighting since before I found the Underlayer. They'll come. Rook's been in Toronto doing recon for months — he knows corporate security. Keiko's fast enough to outrun anything the Hounds can deploy. And Gabriel spent two years inside a military compound in Colombia. He's survived worse than a corporate fortress."

Wren reached through the network. She felt Milo's restless energy, Datu's steady calm, the fierce brightness of Adaeze in Lagos. Others — quieter, warier, still learning to trust the connection. But all of them ready. All of them waiting for something to do with the power they'd been given.
She sent the plan through the frequency. Not words — intention. Structure. Purpose. A siege conducted through the glitch itself.
The network lit up.

Milo would handle external communications — jamming NeuroScale's ability to call for outside help. Datu would coordinate the global network, maintaining the frequency's stability while Wren focused on the tower. Adaeze, still too new for combat, would monitor the data streams for any sign that Vance was accelerating the Counter-Signal timeline.
The assault team would go in physical. Wren would go in digital. Between them, they'd turn the tower inside out.
Thirty-seven invisibles. One tower. Seven days.
Vance had built her endgame. Now Wren would bring it to her.
